annotations.content: On September 3rd, during the dog attacks, as I was reporting up on the hill, documenting that moment of the desecration of sacred land, the Dakota access pipeline jumping forward 20 miles, insulting the people, hurting the women, hurting the elders, to see the ancestors' land being desecrated.

The goal is to educate and empower indigenous folks in most. A job enrolled there was to educate and empower not just our indigenous people but also non-native relatives, they come in solidarity, come to camp, they come to learn, they come to find their own spirit and connection to the earth. And hopes. And there are also (?? tbd) and let go some of their pain and hurt in their history. And their family history. And the US history.

I think it's important to educate yourself about the indigenous people. The  (tbd native laguages names:)  "Piyucheshonee and Washow". In Oklahoma the "Traleteers". "Tukomsa", Sitting Bull... Your contribution will be when you start to see us fighting for the land you'll understand why. Even if it's for clean water as you see. Because, for us, we're connected to the earth and that's why we protect the earth where we're at, and protect our food source, our water, our medecines, for future generations. When you're disconnected, the Dakota access pipeline is disconnected, they can desecrate the land with no consequence in their mind. But there is that spiritual consequence. That emptiness that they have. That's the consequence. That's the result.
The reason I came here was to protect the water, to protect future generations. I feel that it's my duty as a big sister to stand up for the children.

When I first saw a picture of one of my friends - he had locked himself down to a piece of equipment - it was like an invitation for me to finally like... be involved in all the things I feel strongly about and defending what I believe in.

Right before I got here I had a dream about my mom who had passed away and my dreamer mom had asked me for water that I was unable to give her and so I just woke up and started fundraising to bring supplies to Standing Rock.

There are spiritual people all over the world talking about what's happening to their communities, their territory.. As we stand here at Standing Rock we're up against a corrupt system because all my life now we pray for the health and the well-being of our people but we never got that. We see the abuse they are putting on our reservation. Our reservations were concentration camps hundred years ago and we are trying to heal as a nation.

This fight we see here, what's happening here on the ground on Standing Rock's nation, it matters, to fight this pipeline. To the communities here, to the people here. But it also matters because it connects to the greater struggle for us to protect mother earth and to protect our future generations from destructive climate change. When we talk about this pipeline we have to talk about where the oil is coming from. When we talk about where the oil is coming from we have to talk about the man camps that are there that threaten the very lifes of our women and children. That's the thing! The climate change isn't just about the earth, isn't just about the environment, climate change is about our relationship to each other and how we treat each other, it's about climate justice. That's what it's about! Justice!
